Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival verb phrase with object noun and instrumental complement; metaphorical fixed predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To endure hardship with firm courage and not give up when circumstances are heavy.
Usage Contexts: family advice; grief; work pressure; community hardship; encouragement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Evokes the toughness of stone and the resilience expected in Hwange households facing drought, mining-town pressures, and long journeys.
First Example Sentence: Mai vakati, “Sunga moyo nebwe, mwana wangu, basa rinorema asi rinopera.”
Second Example Sentence: Takasunga moyo nebwe pataimirira mvura gore riya.
Third Example Sentence: Kana mhuri yabatwa nenzara, mukuru anofanira kusunga moyo nebwe.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival transitive verb phrase; object noun followed by genitive locative complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To travel far, struggle, or work hard before reaching a goal.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kufamba kure kana kushanda nesimba kuti ubudirire.
Usage Contexts: travel; job searching; migration; school; perseverance
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on dusty rural roads between villages, markets, schools, and workplaces around Hwange.
First Example Sentence: Baba vakadya guruva remugwagwa vasati vawana basa kuWange.
Second Example Sentence: Usaseka munhu ari kudya guruva remugwagwa achitsvaga upenyu.
Third Example Sentence: Vadzidzi vedu vakadya guruva remugwagwa kusvika pachikoro panguva.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival verb phrase with plural object and instrumental noun complement; ironic fixed expression.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To deliberately ignore advice, warnings, or public concern.
Usage Contexts: family correction; politics; workplace warnings; sarcasm
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses the basket as a familiar household object; the image exaggerates how someone blocks useful counsel.
First Example Sentence: Mukoma akavhara nzeve nedengu, zvino dambudziko ramuwana.
Second Example Sentence: Musangano wose wakataura, asi mutungamiri akavhara nzeve nedengu.
Third Example Sentence: Usavhara nzeve nedengu kana vakuru vachikuraira.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival intransitive verb phrase with associative complement; animal-image metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To live or act under the protection, fear, or influence of a powerful person.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kutsigirwa kana kutyisidzirwa nesimba remunhu mukuru.
Usage Contexts: leadership; clan politics; patronage; workplace hierarchy; literature
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The lion image recalls bush landscapes and chiefly authority, where power can protect but also intimidate.
First Example Sentence: Jaya iro rinofamba nomumvuri weshumba, nokuti sabhuku anomutsigira.
Second Example Sentence: Munyori akaratidza vanhu vanofamba nomumvuri weshumba pasina rusununguko.
Third Example Sentence: Kana uchifamba nomumvuri weshumba, usakanganwa inzwi rako.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival transitive verb phrase with instrument complement; impossible-action metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To waste effort on a method that cannot solve the problem.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Based on household sieving and carrying vessels; it humorously contrasts correct tools with futile action.
First Example Sentence: Kugadzirisa nyaya iyi nekupopota chete kusefa mvura netswanda.
Second Example Sentence: Takasefa mvura netswanda patakatenga mbeu tisina kubvunza varimi.
Third Example Sentence: Mudzidzisi akati nzira yedu yaiva yokusefa mvura netswanda.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival intransitive verb phrase with locative complement; hyperbolic metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To stay in a dangerous situation knowingly, often because of pride or stubbornness.
Usage Contexts: relationships; debt; politics; youth advice; conflict mediation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The hearth is central to home life; sleeping on fire reverses comfort into avoidable danger.
First Example Sentence: Wakamuyambira nezvechikwereti, asi akaramba achivata pamoto.
Second Example Sentence: Musikana haafaniri kuvata pamoto nekuda kwerudo runomurwadzisa.
Third Example Sentence: Kambani yakavata pamoto payakaramba kuteerera vashandi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival transitive verb phrase with locative complement; agricultural metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To offer good advice, teaching, or help where it is unlikely to be received or grow.
Usage Contexts: education; parenting; church; mentoring; social criticism
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in dryland farming experience, where soil, timing, and attention determine whether seed grows.
First Example Sentence: Kudzidzisa mwana asingadi kunzwa kukanda mbeu pamabwe.
Second Example Sentence: Vakadzi vemusha vakarega kukanda mbeu pamabwe mushure mekunge zano ravo rarambwa.
Third Example Sentence: Usati wakanda mbeu pamabwe, tanga watsvaga mwoyo unoteerera.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival transitive verb phrase with object noun and locative plural complement; concealed-state metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To hide anger, knowledge, love, or a plan until the right moment.
Usage Contexts: family tension; romance; strategy; leadership; storytelling
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: In rural homes, embers can be kept under ash for later use; the image suggests quiet hidden power.
First Example Sentence: Ambuya vakachengeta moto mumadota, vakazotaura chokwadi musi wemutongo.
Second Example Sentence: Mukomana aichengeta moto mumadota asi maziso ake aitaura rudo.
Third Example Sentence: Mukuru ane njere anochengeta moto mumadota kusvikira nguva yakwana.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival transitive verb phrase with relative clause modifying object; moral metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To betray or harm the person, family, or institution that once helped you.
Usage Contexts: family disputes; workplace loyalty; politics; moral teaching; public criticism
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Trees are valued for shade in hot places; cutting such a tree dramatizes ingratitude.
First Example Sentence: Akasiya vanhu vakamudzidzisa, zvino anonzi atema muti wakamupa mumvuri.
Second Example Sentence: Usatema muti wakakupa mumvuri nekuti nzira dzinotenderera.
Third Example Sentence: Dare rakashora murume akatema muti wakamupa mumvuri.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Infinitival intransitive verb phrase with associative plural noun complement; poetic temporal image.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To speak very early, secretly, or before others are ready to hear the matter.
Usage Contexts: household planning; courtship; village meetings; secrecy; poetic narration
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Morning bird calls mark the beginning of daily life; the phrase links privacy, dawn, and preparation.
First Example Sentence: Vakuru vakataura neshiri dzemangwanani vasati vashevedza musha.
Second Example Sentence: Amai vanoda kutaura neshiri dzemangwanani kana nyaya iri yevana.
Third Example Sentence: Vaviri ava vakataura neshiri dzemangwanani kuti vasavhiringidzwe.
